coredata.dc:title: Green hydrothermal synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from cassava pulps for metal sensing, antioxidant, and mercury detoxification in plants

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been attracted to nanocarbon materials for metal ion sensing, biological activity, and plant phytotoxicity due to their excellent photophysical properties, such as low cytotoxicity, high quantum yield, tunable fluorescence emission, and biocompatibility. Cassava pulp, which consists mainly of starch, has been identified as a low-cost biomass waste from the cassava starch industry. Therefore, this research developed CDs and nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) from cassava pulp using a one-step hydrothermal process in deionized water at 200 °C. The effects of the synthesis conditions, including reaction time (6–24 h) and the nitrogen doping derivatives, were also investigated. CDs and ethylenediamine doped-NCDs exhibited tunable fluorescence emission, strong quantum yield, high photostability, and tolerance to photobleaching. Furthermore, the potential applications of CDs-12 h were demonstrated such as fluorescent sensors for metal ion sensing, antioxidant activity, and mercury detoxification in plants. Fluorescence quenching of the CDs-12 h via both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms was observed in the presence of several metal ions such as Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ with the detection limit in micromolar levels and further applied to real water samples with good recovery and acceptable relative standard derivation. The paper test strip coated with CDs-12 h could also detect these metal ions under UV light. CDs and NCDs-EDA also showed potential DPPH radical scavenging activity and alleviated mercury toxicity in the Chinese cabbage seedlings with the incubation of CDs-12 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h (30 mg/L).

head.ce:title: Green hydrothermal synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from cassava pulps for metal sensing, antioxidant, and mercury detoxification in plants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been attracted to nanocarbon materials for metal ion sensing, biological activity, and plant phytotoxicity due to their excellent photophysical properties, such as low cytotoxicity, high quantum yield, tunable fluorescence emission, and biocompatibility. Cassava pulp, which consists mainly of starch, has been identified as a low-cost biomass waste from the cassava starch industry. Therefore, this research developed CDs and nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) from cassava pulp using a one-step hydrothermal process in deionized water at 200 °C. The effects of the synthesis conditions, including reaction time (6–24 h) and the nitrogen doping derivatives, were also investigated. CDs and ethylenediamine doped-NCDs exhibited tunable fluorescence emission, strong quantum yield, high photostability, and tolerance to photobleaching. Furthermore, the potential applications of CDs-12 h were demonstrated such as fluorescent sensors for metal ion sensing, antioxidant activity, and mercury detoxification in plants. Fluorescence quenching of the CDs-12 h via both static and dynamic quenching mechanisms was observed in the presence of several metal ions such as Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ with the detection limit in micromolar levels and further applied to real water samples with good recovery and acceptable relative standard derivation. The paper test strip coated with CDs-12 h could also detect these metal ions under UV light. CDs and NCDs-EDA also showed potential DPPH radical scavenging activity and alleviated mercury toxicity in the Chinese cabbage seedlings with the incubation of CDs-12 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h (30 mg/L).

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution has emerged as a serious environmental problem and living organisms, including plants, are caused by the high amounts of heavy metals from industries released into the environment [1]. Mercury, iron, and copper ions are common heavy metal ions causing environmental pollution [2]. The excess risks of mercury in humans, including mercuric, mercury (Hg2+), or organic methylmercury, were lung irritation, diarrhea, DNA and chromosome damage, Down's syndrome, and Chisso-Minamata disease [3]. Plant roots have a high affinity for mercury ions, facilitating their efficient uptake and subsequent quick translocation to the edible portions of plants. The presence of mercury (Hg) in plants can lead to disruptions in various biochemical and physiological processes, including the obstruction of vital functional groups in biomolecules, displacement of essential metal ions from biomolecules found in photosynthetic pigments, reduction in photosynthetic rates, and adverse effects on plant nutrient uptake and homeostasis. Consequently, these disturbances can inhibit the growth of roots and shoots, as well as hinder the production of yield [4]. Therefore, metal chelation is one of the key mechanisms involved in mercury detoxification to reduce the accumulation and toxicity of mercury in plants. Furthermore, excess copper (Cu2+) ions cause several diseases in humans, such as hepatocellular degeneration, necrosis, haemolysis, hepatic and kidney disease, or Wilson's disease [5]. An overdose of iron (Fe3+) can induce serious disorders and damage tissues, DNA, proteins, the heart, and the liver or cause carcinogenesis [6]. Metal toxicity, including copper and iron, generates free radical formation via the Fenton reaction, leading to DNA damage and lipid oxidization [3]. The antioxidant agent can against free radical formation triggered by metal ions. Generally, the radical and reactive oxygen species are produced in the biological process. Nevertheless, excessive levels of these species produce oxidative stresses, contributing to several severe diseases and cell damage. Antioxidants can be classified as nonenzymatic (polyphenol, phenolic acids, flavonoids, and ascorbic acid) or enzymatic (superoxide dismutase, catalase, peroxidase, and ascorbate peroxidase) [7].

Carbon dots (CDs), a new zero-dimension nanomaterial in carbon families, have gained significant attention due to their unique properties and potential applications in various fields [8,9]. They have been developed as fluorescent materials for metal ion detections in real water samples or metal detoxification in plants as a result of less cytotoxicity, photostability, resistance to photobleaching, good biocompatibility, high emission quantum yield, and stable physicochemical characteristics [10,11]. Several CDs from natural resources, agricultural waste, and biomass have been developed due to being carbon-rich, renewable, low-cost, and environmentally friendly [12–14]. Generally, biomass-derived CDs have been mainly prepared through hydrothermal methods in deionized water due to their cost-effectiveness, controllability, ease of use, and environmentally friendly nature [15]. The types of precursors and synthesis conditions affect the physical and chemical properties of CDs [16]. In addition, the characteristics of CDs can be substantially changed by heteroatom doping, including nitrogen [17]. The excellent photophysical properties of CDs have been developed as fluorescent sensors for metal ion sensing, such as Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+. For example, lignocellulose-based CDs, such as cassava stem, sorghum stalk, sugarcane bagasse, rubber wood, and poplar, were developed for Fe3+ detection [18]. Recently, CDs from chicken feathers were prepared via the hydrothermal method and further applied as the fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ detection [19].

Cassava (Manihot esculenta) is an important food crop for millions of people in Africa, Asia, and Latin America, providing a valuable source of carbohydrates in their diets. Cassava pulp or cassava residue has been considered a byproduct or waste generated from the cassava starch process. The production of cassava starch factories in Thailand was being raised to a range of 1.5–2.2 million tons per year, and cassava pulp has recently been available for 250–550 THB/ton [20]. Cassava pulp has been widely used for economic and environmental applications, including animal feed, compost and organic fertilizer, and biogas production. Cassava pulp can be utilized as a carbon precursor for carbon dot preparation as it is composed of starch (40.6 %), cellulose (40.4 %), hemicellulose (6.1 %), and lignin (2.0 %) [21]. Cassava peel-derived carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal, microwave, laser ablation, and reflux methods [22–26]. Recently, cassava peels and stretch were synthesized in nitric acid via hydrothermal methods for CDs and supercapacitors [27]. Lignocellulosic-based CDs from cassava stems were developed via hydrothermal carbonization with acid/alkali modifier for drug release [28]. The synthesis of carbon dots from cassava pulp has not been reported as a fluorescent sensor for metal ion detection, antioxidant activity, and mercury detoxification in plants.

Herein, we synthesized CDs and nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) from cassava pulp as a carbon source via an eco-friendly hydrothermal approach. The characterizations of as-synthesized CDs and NCDs were investigated to determine the effect of time synthesis and nitrogen doping. The photophysical, photostability, and photobleaching of CDs and NCDs were also studied. The practical potentials of CDs and NCDs were applied in fluorescent sensors in the solution and on paper-based test strips for sensing metal ions in real water samples. The DPPH antioxidant activity of CDs and NCDs was also performed. Moreover, mercury detoxification in Chinese cabbages by CDs and NCDs was demonstrated in different concentrations.
The dried cassava pulp was purchased from Shopee, Thailand. Polyethyleneimine, branched (MW = 10,000),

o-phenylenediamine, AgOAc, Cu(OAc)2⋅H2O, Pb(OAc)2⋅3H2O, Zn(OAc)2, Co(OAc)2⋅4H2O, Mg(OAc)2⋅4H2O, FeCl3⋅6H2O, and Na2HAsO4⋅7H2O were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Ca(OAc)2⋅H2O, Cd(OAc)2⋅2H2O, and Ni(OAc)2⋅4H2O were purchased from Acros. As2O3, ethylenediamine, quinine hemisulfate monohydrate, and diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl (DPPH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. NaCl was purchased from BDH (Ohio, USA). Hg(OAc)2 was purchased from QREC. A dialysis bag (1000 Da) was purchased from Spectra/Por 6 cellulose dialysis tube. Deionized water (R >18 M Ω cm−1) was used in all experiments.

Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were performed on Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 ATR. The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was collected on the Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD (Shimadzu). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were performed on a JEOL/JEM-2100plus operated at 200 kV. The particle sizes were analyzed by Image J program. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained on a JASCO V-570 UV/Visible spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were collected on a JASCO FP-8500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction patterns were determined using D/teX Ultra 250 X-ray Diffractometer (Rigaku) with Cu-Kα radiation in the range of 5−90° at scan rate of 5.0°/min and step width of 0.02°. Thermogravimetric (TGA) analysis was performed on the thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA 4000, Perkin Elmer, USA) in the temperature of 30–800 °C with the heating rate of 10 °C/min under an N2 atmosphere.

The dried cassava pulp was crushed by a mechanical blender and then sieved (200 mesh) to give a light-brown powder. The cassava pulp powder (4 g) was mixed with deionized water (200 mL) in the hydrothermal Teflon reactor (300 mL), followed by ultra-sonication for 30 min. The resulting mixture was heated in the oven at 200 °C for 6–24 h. The obtained yellow–brown solution was filtrated by Whatman filter paper and 0.2-µm membrane filter, respectively. The supernatant solution of CDs was dialyzed in deionized water by a dialysis bag (1 kDa) for 48 h and then freeze-dried to obtain CDs as a light-brown solid in 31.9 %, 29.7 %, and 16.7 % yield of CDs-6 h, CDs-12 h, and CDs-24 h at 6, 12, and 24 h, respectively.

Several nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs), such as ethylenediamine (EDA) as an aliphatic amine, polyethyleneimine (PEI) as primary, secondary, and tertiary amines, and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as an aromatic amine were also synthesized via hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 6 h. The EDA-doped NCDs (NCDs-EDA) were also synthesized at 200 °C for 6–24 h. The cassava pulp powder (4.0 g) was mixed with the solution of amine derivatives (0.40 g) in deionized water (200 mL), followed by ultra-sonication for 30 min, respectively. The resulting mixture in the hydrothermal Teflon reactor (300 mL) was heated in the oven at 200 °C for 6–24 h. The brown solution was filtrated by Whatman filter paper and 0.2-µm membrane filter, respectively. The supernatant solution of NCDs was dialyzed in deionized water by a dialysis bag (1 kDa) for 72 h and then freeze-dried to obtain NCDs as a brown solid in 24.1 % and 4.1 % yield of NCDs-PEI and NCDs-OPD at 6 h, respectively. NCDs-EDA-6 h, NCDs-EDA-12 h, and NCDs-EDA-24 h were obtained in 24.6 %, 2.6 %, and 5.3 % yield at 6, 12, and 24 h, respectively.

The quantum yield (Φ) of the as-synthesized CDs was determined from the slope of the linear relationship plot of integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance (slope) by comparing quinine sulfate monohydrate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as the reference standard. The stock solution of CDs and NCDs (1.0 mg/mL) was dissolved in deionized water, followed by diluting several concentrations: CDs-6 h (8–24 μg/mL), CDs-12 h (8–24 μg/mL), CDs-24 h (20–100 μg/mL), NCDs-PEI (5–25 μg/mL), NCDs-OPD (5–25 μg/mL), NCDs-EDA-6 h (10–50 μg/mL), NCDs-EDA-12 h (5–40 μg/mL), and NCDs-EDA-24 h (10–100 μg/mL). The quantum yield was calculated according to the following equation:

The stock solutions (10.0 mM) of all metal ions, including Ag+, Na+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, As3+, As5+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ were prepared in deionized water. For the selectivity, the solution of CDs (1.0 mg/mL) in deionized water was observed upon adding metal ions (1.0 mM). Fluorescence intensity was measured by excitation at 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper test strip loaded CDs-12 h
ce:section.ce:para: The paper test strips coated CDs-12 h were prepared by dipping the filter paper (No.1, Whatmann) in the solution of CDs-12 h (1.0 mg/mL) for 5 min. After drying at room temperature, the CDs-12 h coated paper strip was tested with different metal ions (1.0 mM) such as Na+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Co2+, As3+, As5+, Fe3+, Ni2+, and Zn2+. The fluorescence emission of the paper test strip was observed under UV illumination (365 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ in spiked tap water
ce:section.ce:para:

The antioxidant activity of CDs and NCDs was determined by DPPH radical scavenging activity. The solutions of CDs and NCDs (1.0 mg) were prepared in deionized water (500 μL). The DPPH solution (1.0 mM, 250 μL) and methanol (250 μL) were added to the CDs solution. The resulting solution was incubated at ambient temperature for 30 min under dark conditions. After that, the absorbance at 520 nm was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The percent inhibitions of CDs as the standard reference were elucidated in the following equation.
%

Where Acontrol was the absorbance of the DPPH solution, and Asample was the absorbance of the DPPH solution in the addition of the CDs solution.

Seeds of Chinese cabbages (Brassica rapa L.) were germinated for 48 h before the experiment. The seedlings with 5.0 mm of stem length were selected for the experiment and then transferred to 96 well-microplates with three replications of microplates. 150 μL of 0.2 M Hg(OAc)2 and deionized (DI) water as the control were added into each well and then transferred into the growth chamber with a relative humidity of 70 % under visible light at 35 °C for 12 h. After 24 h, all seedlings were washed in deionized water three times. The control seeding was transferred into DI water, and Hg2+-treated seedlings were transferred into the CDs and NCDs-EDA solutions at different concentrations (0, 30, and 60 mg/L). Consequently, microplates were transferred into the growth chamber in the same as in the previous condition. The stem length was measured after seven days. Experimental data were calculated by a one-way ANOVA with DMRT to investigate the statistical differences in CDs and NCDs-EDA using R programming.

The cassava pulp-derived CDs were synthesized in 16.7–31.9 % yield via a facile hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 6–24 h. For the nitrogen-doped CDs, NCDs-EDA-6 h (24.6 % yield) showed the greatest percent yield in the NCDs-PEI (24.1 % yield) and NCDs-OPD (4.1 % yield); however, they were less than CDs-6 h (31.9 % yield) under the same condition. The morphology and size distribution of CDs-12 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h were characterized by HRTEM, as shown in Fig. 1 . Both CDs-12 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h exhibited well-monodispersed and spherical shapes. The diameter distribution histogram of CDs-12 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h represented the average diameter at 2.0 and 11.5 nm within the range of 1–4 and 6–16 nm, respectively. The average size of NCDs-EDA-12 h was greater than that of CDs-12 h due to the growth of the carbon core bonded to nitrogen atoms in condensation and carbonization [29]. High-resolution TEM images of CDs-12 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h revealed lattice fringes of 0.21 and 0.25 nm, respectively, corresponding to the [002] plane of graphitic crystalline structure [17]. The crystallinity of CDs and NCDs-EDA was elucidated by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique (Fig. S1). The XRD patterns of CDs-6 h, CDs-12 h, and CDs-24 h showed the broad peak centered at 2θ = 19.15°, 20.33°, and 20.85°, corresponding to the diffraction of graphite [002] crystal planes, respectively [30]. The weak diffraction peaks of CDs-6 h and CDs-12 h were also found at 41.40° and 38.80°, assigned to the [101] diffraction pattern of graphitic carbon, respectively [31]. The former peak corresponds to the interlayer spacing of CDs-6 h, CDs-12 h, and CDs-24 h were 4.63, 4.36, and 4.26 Å, respectively. For the nitrogen-doped CDs, the [002] diffraction pattern of NCDs-EDA-6 h, NCDs-EDA-12 h, and NCDs-EDA-24 h also showed at 2θ = 17.93°, 19.66°, and 18.80°, respectively. Furthermore, the [002] interlayer spacing of NCDs-EDA-6 h, NCDs-EDA-12 h, and NCDs-EDA-24 h were 4.94, 4.51, and 4.73 Å, respectively. It demonstrated that their inner layer spacings were greater than the spacing of [002] graphite nature (4.12 Å), corresponding to turbostratic carbon structures of CDs and NCDs-EDA [31].

The functional groups of the as-synthesized CDs via the hydrothermal were investigated by the FTIR method. Fig. 2 shows that all CDs exhibited hydroxyl groups due to the existence of several characteristic peaks at 3292 cm−1, 1372–1408 cm−1, 1061–1105 cm−1, corresponding to O-H stretching, O-H bending, and C-O stretching vibration, respectively [31]. The peaks at 2920 cm−1, 1567–1576 cm−1, 751–786 cm−1, which are attributed to C-H stretching, conjugated C=C stretching, and C-H bending vibration of the sp2/sp3 hybridized-carbon framework of CDs, respectively [32]. Moreover, the CDs-24 h exhibited the carboxyl groups since the peaks showed at 1684 cm−1, which are assigned to C=O stretching vibration [33]. Furthermore, the amine moiety of all NCDs-EDA was also elucidated since the intense peaks represented at 3292–3332 cm−1 and 1606–1630 cm−1, attributed to the N-H stretching and N-H bending vibration, respectively [34]. The NCDs-EDA-6 h displayed an intense peak at 1022 cm−1 of C-N bending vibration [35]. Both NCDs-EDA-6 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h exhibited the O-H bending vibration at 1373–1405 cm−1. The hydroxyl groups of NCDs-EDA were found due to the O-H bending vibration of phenol at 1363–1378 cm−1 and C-O stretching at 1056–1075 cm−1. The sp2/sp3 hybridized carbons of all NCDs-EDA showed the C-H stretching peak at 2920 cm−1 and the C=C stretching peaks at 1560–1567 cm−1. The NCDs-EDA-6 h also displayed C-H stretching at 1145 cm−1 and C-H bending at 758 cm−1. These results indicated that the functional groups of all CDs were hydroxyl groups and conjugated sp2 carbons, and the amine groups of NCDs-EDA were also presented. To confirm the surface functional groups of CDs, the thermal stability of CDs and NCDs-EDA was explored by TGA method (Fig. S2). TGA curves of CDs-12 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h showed three stepwise degradations to increasing temperatures in the range of 30–800 °C. TGA curves of CDs-12 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h showed that slight weight loss occurred about 2.5 % at 110 °C and 0.5 % at 116 °C, respectively, due to the decomposition of water molecules or the moisture on the CDs [31]. The initial weight loss of CDs-12 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h occurred at 369 °C (32.5 %) and 393 °C (32.7 %), respectively. These may be due to the decomposition of organic functional groups of the surface CDs and NCDs via covalent linkages [36]. Nonetheless, the final weight loss of CDs-12 h and NCDs-EDA-12 h further changed by about 42.7 % and 32.2 % until 800 °C, respectively, probably due to the decomposition of mineral residue contents [37].

In addition, the surface chemical compositions of CDs and NCDs-EDA were evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), as shown in Fig. 3 . The survey spectra of all CDs and NCDs-EDA showed carbon (C1s, 285 eV), nitrogen (N1s, 399 eV), and oxygen (O1s, 532 eV) (Table S1). The oxygen (O1s) content of CDs-6 h (30.2 %) and CDs-12 h (32.5 %) was greater than CDs-24 h (26.7 %). While the carbon (C1s) content of CDs-6 h (46.0 %) was lower than CDs-12 h (60.5 %) and CDs-24 h (66.9 %). In addition, CDs-12 h and CDs-24 h showed nitrogen (N1s) content of 1.3 % and 1.0 % in the survey spectra, respectively. In the case of the NCDs-EDA, the carbon (C1s) and nitrogen (N1s) contents of NCDs-EDA-12 h and NCDs-EDA-24 h also increased, while the oxygen (O1s) content decreased. These results suggest that the time synthesis of CDs and NCDs-EDA affected the chemical compositions, including carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen amounts. The longer time synthesis, the higher carbon and nitrogen components.

From the high-resolution spectra, C1s spectra of all CDs and NCDs-EDA showed C=C/C-C (285 eV), C-N/C-O (286 eV), C=O (288 eV), COOH (289 eV) (Table S2) [38]. Their O1s spectra showed C-OH (531 eV), C-O-C (533 eV), C=O (534 eV) [39]. N1s spectra of CDs and NCDs-EDA except CDs-6 h showed amino N (NH2) (400 eV) and pyrrolic N (C-N-C) (401 eV) [40]. NCDs-EDA-12 h also exhibited pyridine N (-C=N) (399 eV), corresponding to the previous literature [40]. Several chemical structure of nitrogen-doped CDs was reported the embedded moieties, surface functional groups (amines and amides), and heteroaromatic rings (pyridinic, pyrrolic, graphitic N) [41].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs and NCDs in deionized water were investigated in the range of 200–700 nm (Fig. 4 ). The maximum absorption peak of CDs-6 h, CDs-12 h, and CDs-24 h exhibited 246, 256, and 285 nm, respectively. These results indicated that the absorption wavelength of CDs in the range of 200–300 nm was the characteristic transition of π → π* hydrocarbon conjugation [42]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of several NCDs-6 h, such as EDA, PEI, and OPD, were also studied (Fig. 4b). The maximum absorption peaks of NCDs-OPD exhibited a blue-shift at 240 nm compared to CDs-6 h, while the intense peak NCDs-PEI and NCDs-EDA showed a red-shift at 280 and 330 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of NCDs-EDA-12 h showed the absorption peak at 330 nm, a higher intensity than other conditions (Fig. 4c).

To study the photostability and photobleaching of the as-synthesized CDs, the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of CDs and NCDs-EDA was elucidated under UV radiation and visible light, as shown in Fig. 6 . NCDs-EDA-12 h and NCDs-EDA-24 h showed higher stability than CDs and NCDs-EDA-6 h since the F/F0 slightly decreased by about 10 % with the long irradiation time. It is reasonable to assume that water would produce reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH) upon UV exposure under anaerobic conditions, which can readily trigger the degradation of CDs and the photo-oxidation process [44]. These results indicated that CDs and NCDs-EDA resisted photobleaching due to polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in CDs and NCDs-EDA [45]. The photostability of CDs and NCDs-EDA was also studied under visible light at room temperature (Fig. 6c and d). All NCDs-EDA and CDs-6 h exhibited photostability more than CD-12 h and CD-24 h due to the photodegradation of CDs. Upon visible light irradiation, the surface of CDs could occur photogeneration of electrons and holes, leading to the formation of hydroxyl and alkyl radicals [46]. The radical generation in the photodegradation may also relate to the conjugated π-bond, surface defects, and functional groups of CDs, suggesting that nitrogen doping increased photostability and resistance to photobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and high salt concentration on CDs and NCDs-EDA
ce:section.ce:para: The metal ion sensing in biological and environmental applications of CDs and NCDs-EDA could be affected by several parameters, such as pH and ionic strength. The relative fluorescence intensity (F/Fmax) of CDs and NCDs-EDA was determined in different pH buffers and high ionic strength, as shown in Fig. 6. The fluorescence intensity of CDs and NCDs-EDA changed in the different pH solutions. The pH stability of CDs was only exhibited in weak acidic conditions, while NCDs-EDA displayed pH stability in a wide range of acid, neutral, and base conditions. These results suggested that the pH condition affects the fluorescence emission of CDs since the surface functional groups of CDs occurred in the protonation-deprotonation process [47]. In the alkaline condition, the CO– group on the surface NCDs were deprotonated and the stabilization of NCDs was reported through electronic repulsion [45]. The difference in pH medium also leads to a photobleaching process related to fluorescence reduction [44].

Furthermore, the fluorescence emission of CDs and NCDs-EDA in the high salt concentrations (0–2 M) was investigated. The F/F0 of NCDs-EDA slightly decreased because the high ionic strength of salt may promote the aggregation of NCDs-EDA [48]. All CDs and NCDs-EDA did not change significantly under the high concentrations of NaCl, indicating that CDs and EDA-CDs were highly stable in an aqueous solution with high ionic strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential applications of CDs and NCDs-EDA
Fluorescence sensor for Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The performance for metal ion sensing of CDs and NCDs-EDA towards alkaline (Na+), alkaline earth (Ca2+, Mg2+), heavy metal ions (Hg2+, As3+, As5+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Zn2+), transition metal ions (Ag+, Cd2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+) was elucidated (Fig. S7). Fluorescence quenching of CDs-12 h was significantly observed in the presence of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ ions compared to other CDs and NCDs-EDA. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-12 h (F/F0 = 0.2) was significantly quenched by Hg2+ compared to other CDs (F/F0 = 0.4–0.6), indicating the potential fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ detection. In the case of other metal ions, the fluorescence change of CDs-12 h was slightly observed. However, the fluorescence quenching of CDs-12 h was observed by both Fe3+ (F/F0 = 0.3) and Cu2+ (F/F0 = 0.5 ). Therefore, the coexistence of metal ions of CDs-12 h in the presence of Hg2+ was further investigated, as shown in Fig. 10. The fluorescence quenching of CDs-12 h for Hg2+ sensing was also observed with interference metal ions (Fig. 7 ).

As CDs-12 h was selective toward Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ ions, the sensitivity was also investigated. Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-12 h with the different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–5 mM), Cu2+ (0–10 mM), and Fe3+ (0–30 mM) were explored, as shown in Fig. 8 . The fluorescence intensity of CDs-12 h gradually decreased with the increasing concentrations of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+. The good linear detections of Hg2+ in the range of 30–600 μM (y = 8.3239x – 239.89, R2 = 0.9931), Fe3+ in the range of 0–250 μM (y = 8.8718x + 40.617, R2 = 0.9951), and Cu2+ in the range of 0–200 μM (y = 4.5915x + 10.806, R2 = 0.9963) were observed. The limits of detection (LOD) of CDs-12 h for Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ were calculated to be 12.0, 21.7, and 11.2 μM, according to the signal-to-noise ratio of 3, respectively, less than other biomass-derived CDs, as shown in Table 2 . From the linear equation of the Stern-Volmer plot, the quenching constants (K
sv ) for Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ were estimated to be 5.0×103, 2.7×103, and 6.5×102 L.mol−1, respectively (Fig. S8). These results implied that the binding of CDs-12 h and Hg2+ was greater than Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively.

As fluorescence emission of CDs-12 h was quenched upon the addition of Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ ions, the possible fluorescence emission was further proposed, as shown in Fig. 9 . The fluorescence quenching mechanism did not occur via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) because the solution of CDs-12 h in the presence of Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ ions did not show fluorescence emission under UV illumination (365 nm) [42]. For the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs-12 h (0.1 mg/mL) with Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ ions (100 μM), the absorbance slightly increased without any red or blue shift. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ as the quencher overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-12 h, suggesting the fluorescence quenching of CDs-12 h occurred via the inner filter effect (IFE) [30]. The fluorescence quenching also occurred via non-radiative electron transfer from the excited state of surface functional groups, including OH and C=O groups, to the d-orbital of Hg2+, Fe3+, and Cu2+ [49]. In addition, a linear Stern-Volmer plot of Fe3+ was classified as the static or dynamic quenching process. In contrast, the nonlinear Stern-Volmer plots at high concentrations of Hg2+ and Cu2+ were observed for the competitive quenching mechanism between static and dynamic quenching mechanisms [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper test strip of CDs-12 h for visual detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, paper-based portable devices have been greatly interesting due to their potential practicality in field tests. Therefore, the paper test strips loaded CDs-12 h were developed for optical detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 10 . The blue fluorescence emission of the paper test strips coated CDs-12 h was not observed in the dipping of Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+ compared to other metal ions, suggesting that the CDs-12 h based-paper test strips could be applied to detect Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs-12 h in the real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the potential application of CDs-12 h, various concentrations of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in real water samples were detected by fluorescence emission of CDs-12 h. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-12 h was measured in tap water by spiking Hg2+ and Fe3+ (Table 3 ). The recoveries were found to be satisfactory in the range of 97.3–105.6 %), which was in good agreement with the spiked values. The relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was less than 5 %, which was acceptable compared to previous reports [53]. These results indicated that CDs-12 h could have a great potential for detecting Hg2+ and Fe3+ in real water analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant activity of CDs and NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the antioxidant properties of CDs and NCDs, a radical scavenging (DPPH) assay of all CDs (1.0 mg/mL) was investigated, as shown in Table 4 . The DPPH percent inhibitions of CDs-6 h showed higher than other CDs, suggesting that the trend of DPPH inhibition decreased with the higher time synthesis. The trends of percent inhibition of each CDs were in good agreement with the absorption wavelength and relative absorption intensities of the π → π* transition. Moreover, the relative area of C=C/C–C from the high-resolution C1s spectra of CDs-6 h showed higher than CDs-24 h (Table S2). The NCDs-6 h, including PEI, OPD, and EDA, exhibited the DPPH inhibition less than CDs-6 h as a result of the decreasing absorption peaks of the π → π* transition. From the previous literature, the surface functional groups of CDs, including hydroxyl (–OH) affect the antioxidant activity [54]. The possible mechanism of radical scavenging involved in the hydrogen transfer from the surface functional groups of CDs and NCDs to DPPH⋅, including carboxyl (COOH), hydroxyl (OH), and amino (NH2) groups [7]. From the high resolution O1s spectra, the relative area of C-OH of all NCDs-EDA showed less than CDs, leading to the lower DPPH inhibition of CDs.

The consumption of mercury (Hg)-contaminated vegetables is a significant route of Hg exposure for humans. The application of CDs and NCDs-EDA to alleviate mercury toxicity was demonstrated for the mitigation of the harmful effects of mercury on plants. The Chinese cabbage was investigated as a model plant for Hg tolerance because it is a widely consumed and grown leafy vegetable crop in the world [55]. The stem length of the mercuric acetate incubated seedlings was inhibited by at least 25 % by comparing the control group in deionized water. The Hg2+-pretreated seeding was incubated in several concentrations of the CDs and NCDs-EDA solutions (Fig. 11 ). The stem lengths of the seeding pretreated CDs-12 h, NCDs-EDA-12 h, and NCDs-EDA-24 h solutions increased greater than other conditions since the phytotoxicity of Hg2+ decreased by the binding of CDs and NCDs-EDA. In addition, the concentration of CDs-12 h at 30.0 mg/L significantly exhibited the increasing stem length of the Hg2+-pretreated seeding greater than that of NCDs-EDA as the average size of CDs-12 h (2.0 nm) was less than that of NCDs-EDA (11.5 nm). From the fluorescence experiment, the binding capability of CDs-12 h and Hg2+ displayed better than NCDs-EDA (Fig. S6). These results indicated that the size of carbon dots and the binding ability of Hg2+ may affect mercury detoxification in plants. Although the average sizes of CDs and NCDs-EDA were different, they may be an uptake in the plants. The previous literature reported that the average size of CDs less than 50 nm could be uptake in the xylem of plants [56]. Moreover, the Hg2+ stress of the seeding was mitigated by CDs and NCDs due to the free radicals inhibition (Table 2) [57].

In summary, carbon dots (CDs) with/without nitrogen doping (NCDs) were successfully synthesized via a one-step, simple, and environmentally friendly hydrothermal method using cassava pulp as the available carbon precursor. The CDs and NCDs exhibited a monodispersed spherical shape with graphite and amorphous structure due to the surface functional groups, including amino and hydroxyl moieties. The carbon and nitrogen contents of both CDs and NCDs at the longer time synthesis showed higher mass concentrations, while the oxygen content displayed lower mass concentrations. For the photophysical properties, CDs exhibited intense UV absorption peaks of 200–300 nm and NCDs presented red-shift with new absorption peaks of 300–500 nm, according to the π → π* and η → π* transition, respectively. The temperature and time synthesis conditions affected the quantum yield of CDs. The as-synthesized CDs and NCDs showed tunable fluorescence emission in the visible region. The higher time of preparation, the greater the quantum yield. In contrast, the effect of the time synthesis showed no significant difference in the quantum yield of NCDs. CDs and NCDs showed tolerance photobleaching under UV light and high photostability under visible light, different pH, and high salt concentrations. CDs-12 h as the “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for Hg2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ ions exhibited the detection limit of 12.0, 21.7, and 11.2 μM without interference metal ions, respectively. The fluorescence quenching for these ions occurred in the non-radiative electron transfer of CDs. The Hg2+ and Cu2+ sensing was proposed through static and dynamic quenching mechanisms, while Fe3+ sensing was suggested via inner filter effect (IFE) and static or dynamic quenching process. CDs-12 h was further applied to detect Hg2+ and Cu2+ in tap water with a good recovery of 97.3–105.6 % and an acceptable relative standard deviation of 0.70–4.85 %. The blue fluorescence emission of paper test-strip coated CDs-12 h was not observed in the presence of these ions (Fe3+, Hg2+, Cu2+) compared to other metal ions. CDs showed the highest inhibitions of DPPH radical scavenging activity. The as-synthesized CDs-12 h (30 mg/L) and NCDs-EDA (60 mg/L) could be an effective repair agent to reduce Hg2+ toxicity in Chinese cabbage seedlings. From these results, the as-synthesized CDs showed promising utilization in various sensors for metal ions and metal toxicity alleviation in plants.
